_comment: Per-chart narratives. CI (build_prompt.py -> GitHub Models -> write_narrative.py) overwrites these from live numbers; this committed copy is the fallback so the page always reads well. source = 'fallback' | 'github-models'.

notes.fossil: WTI crude trades near $71 a barrel, Brent a few dollars above it at $75, Henry Hub natural gas around $3.30 per MMBtu, and US regular gasoline about $3.10 a gallon. Put all three on one scale (each indexed to its 2019 level) and they track loosely together in calm periods, because they share the same underlying demand for moving things and heating them. The line that breaks ranks is natural gas: it is expensive to ship across oceans, so regional shortages can't be smoothed out the way they can for oil, and that is why it spiked far harder than crude or the pump in 2022 before falling back. Oil is the global price; gas is still, in large part, a local one.
notes.transition: The levelised cost of electricity is the all-in price of a megawatt-hour over a plant's whole life, including what it costs to build, fuel and run, which makes it the fairest way to compare a solar farm with a gas turbine. On that measure new solar PV now lands around $44 per MWh and onshore wind near $33, both comfortably below new coal at roughly $110 and gas combined-cycle at about $76. The decisive move is solar's: its levelised cost has fallen roughly 90% since 2010, from around $460 to the low forties, which is one of the fastest cost declines of any industrial technology on record. The catch the headline number hides is that wind and solar are intermittent, so the system still pays separately for the storage and backup that firm them up.
notes.currency: For half a century almost every barrel of oil was priced and paid for in dollars, and that petrodollar habit is one of the quieter reasons the dollar sits at the centre of global finance. That is still overwhelmingly the case, so this should be read as a shift at the margin rather than a changing of the guard. Since 2022, though, a measurable slice of the trade (an estimate of roughly a fifth, since there is no official figure) has started settling in yuan, rupees and dirhams, driven mainly by Russia rerouting its crude to Asian buyers who would rather not touch the dollar. The direction is consistent even if the exact percentage isn't, and that direction is away from a dollar monopoly.
notes.now: Read together, the three charts say energy's loud story (the day's barrel and the price at the pump) is the least important one. The slower signals are that the cheapest new electricity in most of the world is now renewable, and that the dollar's grip on the oil trade, while still firm, has loosened at the edges. Neither shift is finished, and both matter more for the next decade than this week's crude price.
